106 ("Parents do not have a constitutional due process right to appear at
proceedings to terminate their parental rights, and their due pro
evidence: The PDF source shows '106 (' on the same line (a citation with reporter page followed immediately by opening quote). The DB incorrectly inserted an extra newline between the page number and the quote, creating '106\n ('. This is an ingestion/OCR artifact where an errant line break was added during transcription.
note: Citation fragmentation defect. The PDF layout places '106 (' on the same line. The DB split it with an unnecessary newline. This is a genuine split_join defect, distinct from the deterministic citation-rejoin issues handled by Rule 10, because the source shows these elements on the same line.

source_quote: [¶2] In April of 2019, the Department issued Christianson an order of suspension
evidence: The DB text fuses the section header 'I' with the paragraph marker '[¶2]' into a single token 'I[¶2]'. The source PDF shows these as separate elements: the section header 'I' appears on its own centered line (line 49), followed by a blank line, then '[¶2]' begins the next paragraph (line 51). This is a structural formatting error where the section header was dropped and fused with the paragraph number during ingestion/processing.

source_quote: [¶19] Christianson argues that the Department did not receive notice of the conviction
evidence: Similar to Issue 1: The DB fuses the section header 'IV' with '[¶19]' into 'IV[¶19]'. The source PDF properly separates them with the section header on its own line (line 383) followed by blank line, then '[¶19]' on line 385. Both instances demonstrate a consistent pattern of section headers being wrongly fused with their following paragraph markers during OCR/ingestion.
